children.name: Phalacrocorax
children.description: Great cormorants and shags — large, fish-eating waterbirds whose distinctive habit of spreading their wings to dry after diving is one of the most familiar sights at Swedish coasts and large inland lakes. Cormorants lack the fully waterproofed plumage of ducks and grebes, instead using partially water-permeable feathers that reduce buoyancy and help them pursue fish at depth — but require post-dive drying. The great cormorant (storskarv) is one of the most controversial birds in Sweden: its population exploded from near-extinction to over 120,000 pairs between the 1970s and 2010s, leading to intense conflict with fishermen and aquaculture operators. Large colonies defoliate nesting trees with their corrosive droppings, creating dramatic 'white forests' on Baltic island nature reserves. The European shag is strictly a coastal saltwater species, not venturing onto the Baltic.

children.name: Phalacrocorax carbo
children.description: The storskarv — one of Sweden's most dramatic conservation success stories turned management challenge. Reduced to a handful of pairs in the 1960s due to persecution and pollution, the population rebounded explosively after DDT bans and protection, reaching over 120,000 breeding pairs. Colonies on Baltic islands kill surrounding trees with nitrogen-rich droppings, creating distinctive 'white forests'. Highly efficient fish predators — a single bird can eat 500–700 g of fish daily — making them the subject of intense fisheries conflict. The post-dive wing-spreading posture is their most recognisable behaviour.

children.name: Phalacrocorax aristotelis
children.description: The toppskarv — smaller and more iridescent than the great cormorant, with a distinctive forward-curling crest in breeding season. Strictly a saltwater species in Sweden, breeding on rocky sea cliffs on the Kattegat coast and a few offshore islands; never ventures onto fresh water or the low-salinity Baltic. Its emerald-green iridescent plumage and yellow gape distinguish it clearly from the cormorant. Dives in shallower, rockier water than the great cormorant, targeting sand eels, sprats, and gobies. Sweden is at the northeastern limit of its European breeding range.

children.name: Microcarbo
children.description: Pygmy cormorants — small, long-tailed cormorants of freshwater lakes and rivers in southeastern Europe and central Asia. The pygmy cormorant (dvärgskarv) is the smallest European cormorant, barely larger than a moorhen, and inhabits reed-fringed lakes, river deltas, and wetlands from the Balkans and Turkey east through the Caspian region. It is a vagrant of increasing regularity to Sweden as the species expands its range northward from the Danube delta and the Balkans following decades of population growth. Sightings have been recorded at Swedish wetlands in autumn and winter, typically among roosting flocks of great cormorants, where the pygmy cormorant's small size and longer tail and neck make it detectable to the observant birder.

children.name: Microcarbo pygmaeus
children.description: The dvärgskarv — a rare vagrant to Sweden from southeastern Europe, recorded with increasing frequency as the Balkan population expands. Much smaller than the great cormorant with a proportionally longer tail that it often cocks upward, and a relatively smaller, rounder head. In breeding plumage the dark chestnut-brown head is spotted white. Typically found among cormorant roosts at Swedish freshwater sites or coastal bays in autumn and winter. Breeds in dense reedbeds at Danube delta and Balkan lakes in colonies that number hundreds of thousands.
